sanskrit_devanagari:
इन्द्रियाणां हि चरतां यन्मनोऽनुविधीयते ।
तदस्य हरति प्रज्ञां वायुर्नावमिवाम्भसि ॥ ६७ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
indriyāṇāṁ hi caratāṁ yan mano 'nuvidhīyate |
tad asya harati prajñāṁ vāyur nāvam ivāmbhasi || 67 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (canonical body text; identical word order indriyāṇāṁ hi caratāṁ / yan mano 'nuvidhīyate / tad asya harati prajñāṁ / vāyur nāvam ivāmbhasi)
exact (text body identical; punctuation rendering differs — single danda vs double danda)
exact (text body identical; verse-number marker rendered as ।।2.67।।)
IAST exact (Devanagari not displayed; IAST body matches)
exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_02.067 = MBh_06,024.067). Body text identical.

anvaya.meaning: in the water / on the waters

translations.verbatim_quote: As a strong wind sweeps away a boat on the water, even one of the senses on which the mind focuses can carry away a man's intelligence.
Just as a strong wind sweeps a boat off its chartered course on the water, even one of the senses on which the mind focuses can lead the intellect astray.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Unless all of the senses are engaged in the service of the Lord, even one of them engaged in sense gratification can deviate the devotee from the path of transcendental advancement. As mentioned in the life of Mahārāja Ambarīṣa, all of the senses must be employed in Kṛṣṇa consciousness, for that is the correct technique for controlling the mind.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Even a single sense, if not yoked, drags the mind, and through the mind, intelligence (prajñā) wanders off course. The mind is the boat; the wind is any one un-restrained sense; the water is the field of action. Total engagement of all senses in devotional service is offered as the correct yoke.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Hi, for; yat manas, that mind which; anuvidhiyate, becomes engaged; in following indriyanam, the senses; caratam, that move among their objects, which (mind) becomes engaged in thinking of the qualities of those (sense-objects); tat, that (mind); harati, deprives, takes away; asya, his, the yogi's who is striving for liberation; prajnam, knowledge — the discriminating knowledge that has arisen with regard to the Self and the not-Self.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads prajñā specifically as ātma-anātma-viveka — the discriminating knowledge that distinguishes Self from not-Self. The mind that follows even one wandering sense erodes this knowledge in the very yogi who has begun to attain it. The verse is the negative side of 2.68's positive statement: where 2.68 names what stable prajñā looks like, 2.67 names the precise mechanism by which it is lost — mind-following-sense as the prajñā-thief.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: summary-captured (page renders bhashya in JS; paraphrase recovered)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: When the mind follows even a single sense roving among its objects, it draws the discrimination of the aspirant away — as the wind, by entering the sail, drags a boat off its desired course on the waters. Hence the absolute necessity of withdrawing the senses for one who would meditate upon the Self and the Lord.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja preserves the metaphor structurally: the boat is the aspirant's prajñā moving toward its goal (the Lord); the wind is the un-yoked sense that intrudes through the mind. The verse is presented as the engineering necessity behind sense-withdrawal — not asceticism for its own sake, but the structural prevention of course-deviation. The Vishishtadvaita reading specifically locates the goal as devotion to the Supreme; the wind-boat metaphor is the same.

positions.rendering: discriminating knowledge of Self and not-Self (ātma-anātma-viveka)
the aspirant's discrimination directed toward the Lord

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All four agree prajñā here is the higher discriminative faculty, not mere cognition. They diverge on what prajñā discriminates: Shankara names the Self / not-Self distinction (Advaita's central viveka); Ramanuja names the aspirant's directed discrimination toward the Supreme; Prabhupada and Mukundananda translate broadly as 'intelligence' / 'intellect', losing the specifically yogic technical sense. The wind-boat metaphor is preserved identically across all four.

positions.rendering: becomes engaged in following / thinking of the qualities of sense-objects

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: anuvidhīyate is a passive of vi-dhā with anu- prefix: 'is led along after / is regulated by'. The mind is the subject — it is the one being led, not the one leading. Shankara expands the passive: the mind that becomes engaged in (anu-) following the senses' contemplation of their objects' qualities. The English translations preserve the directionality but compress to 'focuses on', losing the sense that the mind is the passive party here. This matters: the verse is naming a passivity-failure, not an active grasping.

literal_meaning: For when the mind follows the wandering senses, that mind carries away his discriminative wisdom — like the wind a boat upon the waters.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna gives the structural mechanism by which sense-control matters: it is not that uncontrolled senses are merely unaesthetic — it is that the un-yoked mind, drawn behind even one of them, actively erodes prajñā, the discriminating knowledge the yogi has been cultivating. The wind-boat metaphor is concrete: the boat is moving across waters with intent (the aspirant moving toward the goal); the wind that enters does not need to be a storm — even a single un-restrained sense is enough to deflect the course. Shankara reads prajñā as ātma-anātma-viveka. Ramanuja reads it as the aspirant's directed discrimination. Both agree the verse names a precise causal chain: sense-object → un-yoked mind → drawn-along consciousness → eroded prajñā. This verse is the negative case-statement; the very next verse (2.68) restates the positive — whose senses are restrained from sense-objects, their prajñā is established.

verbatim_quote_sources: bhagavad-gita.us (Ramanuja Gita-bhasya, Adidevananda tr. — paraphrase from JS-rendered page)
remaining_gaps: Ramanuja verbatim — page renders commentary in JS; only paraphrase recovered.
